title: Everyone's Watching NVIDIA. The Real AI Play Is the Company Plugging Everything In.
subtitle: GE Vernova sold every gas turbine slot through 2030. Its $163B backlog grew 32% in a year. This is the infrastructure layer underneath the AI boom that nobody's pricing in.
summary: GE Vernova's $163B backlog, $2.4B in single-quarter data center orders, and a sold-out-through-2030 gas turbine lineup make it the overlooked infrastructure backbone of the AI revolution. With $7.5B in free cash flow, a pristine balance sheet, and a three-company oligopoly in utility-scale turbines, GEV is the pick-and-shovel play behind every hyperscaler buildout.

image.caption: GE Vernova's HA gas turbines are the backbone of AI data center power infrastructure.

links.label: Bloomberg — AI Boom Shifts GE Vernova's Customer Mix

videos.title: AI Boom Changing GE Vernova's Customer Mix, CEO Says

bodyHtml:
You've been watching the wrong stock.

Every day it's the same show. NVIDIA's next GPU. Microsoft's next data center. Which hyperscaler is spending the most on AI this quarter. And sure, those are important stories. But here's the thing nobody wants to admit: none of it works without power. Not a single GPU cluster, not one inference call, nothing. You can stack H100s to the ceiling, but if the lights don't turn on, you've got the world's most expensive paperweight collection.

That's where GE Vernova comes in. Market cap: $293 billion. Revenue: $39 billion and growing 16% year-over-year. Free cash flow: $7.5 billion. And here's the number that should make you sit up — $163 billion in total backlog, up from $123 billion a year ago. That's four-plus years of revenue, already contracted, already in the pipe. And the company's sold every single gas turbine slot through 2030. Every. Single. One.

So what flipped the switch? AI data centers. In Q1 2026 alone, GEV's electrification segment booked $2.4 billion in data center equipment orders. Let that sink in: that's more data center-related orders in three months than in all of 2025 combined. The gas turbine reservation backlog hit 100 gigawatts — up from 83 GW in a single quarter. The orders are crashing in faster than they can stamp them out. Electrification segment orders surged 71% organically. This isn't a gradual ramp — it's a hockey stick.

CEO Scott Strazik laid it out flat: "We're selling the gas turbines, but we're also selling the electrical equipment, the substation equipment, and the software to help them manage the load." That's the full-stack play nobody else can replicate. Siemens Energy can give you a turbine. Mitsubishi Heavy can give you a turbine. Neither of them hands you the switchgear, the transformers, the substation, and the software to run it all. GEV is the only company that owns the entire energy stack from combustion to connection.

That's why analysts are pounding the table. Thirty-four analysts cover GEV and the consensus is a Strong Buy, with an average price target around $1,223 — roughly 12% upside from today's $1,091. Institutions own about 80% of the float. The balance sheet is pristine: $10.2 billion in cash against just $2.8 billion in debt. Near-zero net debt. Forward P/E around 45x — not cheap, but you're paying for an oligopoly in a world that's suddenly desperate for power.

Because here's the structural reality that matters: GE Vernova, Siemens Energy, and Mitsubishi Heavy are the only three companies on earth that can build utility-scale HA gas turbines at scale. That's it. Three companies control the global supply. And they're all at absolute max capacity. If you want a turbine for your data center, you get in line. The line currently stretches past 2030. Pricing is up more than 300% since 2023. When your customers are hyperscalers who literally cannot afford to wait for power, you get to name your price.

Look, let's keep it real about the risks. The wind segment has been a persistent drag — the Vineyard Offshore wind blade failure and ensuing disputes haven't helped. Offshore wind is a small part of the narrative now, but it's still bleeding roughly $600 million in EBITDA losses. And at 45x forward earnings, there's zero margin for error in the multiple. If the AI buildout decelerates, if regulators start squeezing data center emissions, or if GEV stumbles on execution — that multiple compresses in a hurry.

But the bigger picture is undeniable. AI hyperscaler capex is projected to hit $750 billion in 2026, heading toward $1 trillion in 2027. The Department of Energy says data centers could consume 12% of U.S. electricity by 2028 — up from roughly 4% today. Every gigawatt of IT load needs about 2.5 GW of generating capacity behind it for reliability. The math is brutally simple: more AI means more data centers, more data centers means more power demand, and more power demand means more turbines. And GEV is the only company that can sell you the turbine, the grid equipment, and the software to manage it all under one roof.

Everyone's been watching NVIDIA's GPU shipments and the hyperscaler capex arms race. Maybe it's time to look at what's actually plugging into the wall. Because the AI revolution doesn't run on chips. It runs on electrons. And GE Vernova owns the pipes.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in GEV. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
